name: Brazil
card_title: Executive Lobbying and the Quest for Autonomy
card_blurb: Brazil is Starlink's second-largest global market, with 250,000 active users and 85% annual growth in 2025. Despite strong urban penetration, only 22% of Brazilians meet 'meaningful connectivity' standards.
header_info: 5 providers authorized · 250,000 Starlink subscribers · R$102,677 licensing fee until 2027
key_finding: R$102,677 in licensing fees for rights worth billions — less than R$5 per satellite per year

primary_driver.label: Executive Lobbying
primary_driver.description: The authorization was actively engineered by the Bolsonaro government, which lobbied the regulator and met directly with SpaceX weeks before the regulatory framework was active.

local_presence.label: Local Subsidiary
local_presence.description: Starlink operates via Starlink Brazil Holding Ltda. and holds an exploitation right for spectrum and a retail broadband license.

competition.label: Near-Monopoly
competition.description: While five providers are authorized on paper, the market is in practice a near-monopoly where Starlink is the only operational consumer LEO player at scale.

key_gap.label: Digital Sovereignty
key_gap.description: The framework initially lacked data localization requirements, space sustainability rules, and mechanisms for state control during geopolitical disputes.

summary: Brazil is Starlink's second-largest global market, with 250,000 active users and 85% annual growth in 2025. Despite 85% urban internet penetration, only 22% of Brazilians meet 'meaningful connectivity' standards, exposing a sharp class divide: 83% of Class A households are meaningfully connected versus just 1% of Class DE. The government granted Starlink exploitation rights for R$102,677 until 2027 — less than R$5 per satellite per year — raising serious questions about regulatory capture. The 2024 X/Twitter ban crisis, in which Musk threatened to defy Brazilian court orders, prompted the reactive authorization of SpaceSail as a geopolitical hedge. Meanwhile, 50 Starlink terminals were seized from illegal mining operations in Yanomami indigenous territory, illustrating the governance vacuum around infrastructure misuse.

market_structure.licensing_pathway_note: RGSat (Resolution 748/2021) — authorization process heavily influenced by political lobbying from the Bolsonaro government; a ten-day public comment window applied to exploitation rights applications, very short by comparative regulatory standards.

governance_scorecard.summary_note: 1 of 7 governance indicators met. A ten-day public comment window existed but was criticized as too brief; no cybersecurity, QoS, or data residency obligations were included.

red_flags.text: Licensing fee of R$102,677 for exploitation rights until 2027 — less than R$5 per satellite per year
Authorization process influenced by political lobbying; 8 formal government meetings with Starlink in one year
50 Starlink terminals seized from illegal mining and deforestation operations in Yanomami territory
SpaceSail (China) authorized as reactive geopolitical hedge — not through structured sovereignty policy
Meaningful connectivity gap: 83% Class A vs 1% Class DE — LEO growth not reaching the most underserved

policy_levers: Multi-constellation mandate to reduce single-provider dependency
Fair contribution mechanism to Universal Service Funds
Meaningful connectivity standards linked to licensing conditions
